key_facts: Reported in Kozhikode and Malappuram districts in May 2018. Fruit bats (flying foxes) were the suspected reservoir. Hospital-acquired infections occurred among healthcare workers.
response_summary: WHO did not recommend travel restrictions. Rapid response team deployed for contact tracing and isolation. Healthcare facilities enhanced infection prevention measures.
lessons_learned: Early outbreak response required improvisation. Surveillance personnel needed additional training in field epidemiology. Data sharing between India and WHO was limited, missing opportunities for clinical trials.
context_snippet: Healthcare workers and community members in Kerala affected by Nipah virus with very high fatality rate, linked to fruit bat reservoir and hospital transmission.

key_facts: Primarily pneumonic plague affecting urban areas including Antananarivo and Toamasina. Unusual for its rapid urban spread and human-to-human transmission pattern.
response_summary: WHO assessed high risk for Madagascar spread, moderate risk for regional spread, low global risk. International support provided for case management and vector control.
lessons_learned: Optimal climate conditions for rat fleas facilitated transmission. High reproduction number (5-7) demonstrated potential for rapid spread even with low initial exposure rates.
context_snippet: Pneumonic plague spread rapidly through urban Madagascar, with human-to-human respiratory transmission and high reproduction number in dense city populations.

key_facts: Vector-borne outbreak spread by Aedes mosquitos. Cross-border transmission from Angola to DRC. Urban transmission pattern in previously unprotected populations.
response_summary: WHO declined to declare PHEIC in May and August. Mass vaccination campaign administered ~7 million doses, exhausting global vaccine supply by September.
lessons_learned: Global vaccine supply insufficient for pandemic-scale response. Under-resourced countries and limited production capacity remain critical vulnerabilities for epidemic preparedness.
context_snippet: Yellow fever spread across Angola and into DRC via Aedes mosquitos, triggering a mass vaccination campaign that exhausted the entire global vaccine stockpile.

key_facts: Outbreak enabled by dropped pediatric vaccination rates following anti-vaccine activism. Part of broader Pacific region measles epidemic affecting Tonga, Fiji, Philippines, and New Zealand.
response_summary: Emergency vaccination campaign achieved 94% coverage by December 2019. State of emergency declared to facilitate vaccination efforts.
lessons_learned: Measles requires high vaccination coverage (95%+) due to extremely high transmissibility (R0: 12-18). Anti-vaccination misinformation poses serious public health threat.
context_snippet: Measles outbreak in Samoa fueled by anti-vaccine activism that reduced childhood vaccination coverage, killing 83 people — mostly children — in a preventable epidemic.

key_facts: First pandemic influenza of 21st century. Initial case in La Gloria, Veracruz. Estimated 700M-1.4B total cases globally. Affected previously healthy individuals.
response_summary: WHO declared pandemic in June 2009. Global vaccine development and distribution efforts. Later criticized for potential overreaction given severity estimates.
lessons_learned: Pandemic preparedness systems activated effectively but severity assessment proved challenging in real-time. Livestock culling (Egypt) was an unnecessary overreaction.
context_snippet: Novel H1N1 influenza emerged in Mexico and spread globally as the first 21st-century pandemic, with final severity lower than initially feared by health authorities.

key_facts: First pandemic coronavirus. Originated from animal markets in Guangdong. Super-spreading events in hospitals and hotels amplified transmission.
response_summary: WHO issued global alerts and travel advisories. International containment efforts including contact tracing, isolation, and quarantine measures.
lessons_learned: Demonstrated importance of rapid information sharing and international coordination. Hospital infection control critical for respiratory pathogens.
context_snippet: Novel coronavirus emerged from animal markets in China causing severe respiratory illness, spreading internationally via superspreading events in hospitals and hotels.

key_facts: Ongoing sporadic cases since 2012 with dromedary camels as reservoir. Largest outbreak in South Korea (2015) with 186 cases from single importation.
response_summary: Enhanced surveillance systems established in Middle East. Camel vaccination programs initiated. Hospital infection control strengthened.
lessons_learned: Zoonotic spillover events can continue for years. Healthcare-associated transmission amplifies outbreaks significantly. One Health approaches essential.
context_snippet: Middle East respiratory coronavirus with dromedary camel reservoir causing ongoing sporadic spillovers and healthcare-associated clusters since 2012.

key_facts: Largest Ebola outbreak in history. Spread to urban areas for first time. International cases in US and Europe prompted global response.
response_summary: WHO declared PHEIC in August 2014. International military and medical assistance deployed. Experimental treatments and vaccines developed.
lessons_learned: Weak health systems amplify epidemic potential. Cultural practices around burial require sensitive intervention. International coordination essential for containment.
context_snippet: Largest Ebola outbreak in history swept across West Africa, spreading to urban areas and triggering a PHEIC as fragile health systems collapsed under the caseload.

key_facts: First global mpox outbreak outside endemic regions. Primarily affected men who have sex with men networks. Previously called monkeypox.
response_summary: WHO declared PHEIC in July 2022. Targeted vaccination campaigns in affected communities. Enhanced surveillance and laboratory capacity.
lessons_learned: Pathogens can emerge in unexpected populations and transmission patterns. Community engagement and targeted interventions more effective than broad measures.
context_snippet: Mpox virus emerged globally outside its endemic range, spreading primarily through close contact in sexual networks and prompting a WHO PHEIC declaration.

key_facts: Linked to microcephaly in newborns and Guillain-Barré syndrome. Spread rapidly across Americas via Aedes aegypti mosquitos.
response_summary: WHO declared PHEIC in February 2016. Pregnancy guidance issued globally. Vector control and travel advisories implemented.
lessons_learned: Mild infections can have severe consequences in vulnerable populations. Pregnancy complications require special public health consideration.
context_snippet: Zika virus spread across the Americas via Aedes mosquitos, causing mild illness in most adults but severe congenital microcephaly in newborns of infected pregnant women.

key_facts: Highly pathogenic avian influenza with sporadic human infections. Recent emergence in dairy cattle (2024) raised pandemic concerns.
response_summary: Enhanced surveillance in poultry and dairy industries. Pandemic preparedness planning activated. Vaccine development accelerated.
lessons_learned: Cross-species transmission events require immediate attention. Agricultural workers at highest risk. Pandemic potential remains concerning.
context_snippet: Highly pathogenic H5N1 avian influenza continues causing sporadic high-fatality human cases, with recent spread into US dairy cattle raising pandemic concern.

key_facts: Most significant pandemic since 1918 influenza. Global economic and social disruption. Multiple variants of concern emerged.
response_summary: WHO declared pandemic March 2020. Unprecedented global lockdowns, vaccine development in record time, international coordination efforts.
lessons_learned: Early detection and transparency critical. Non-pharmaceutical interventions buy time for medical countermeasures. International cooperation essential but fragile.
context_snippet: SARS-CoV-2 emerged in Wuhan causing the most disruptive global pandemic in a century, with airborne transmission driving rapid spread and overwhelming health systems worldwide.

key_facts: First reported Marburg outbreak in Equatorial Guinea. Fruit bats suspected reservoir. High case fatality rate typical for filovirus.
response_summary: WHO deployed rapid response teams. Contact tracing and isolation measures implemented. Laboratory capacity strengthened.
lessons_learned: Filovirus outbreaks require immediate aggressive response. Fruit bat ecology linked to spillover events. Healthcare worker protection critical.
context_snippet: First-ever Marburg virus outbreak recorded in Equatorial Guinea, with a very high fatality rate and suspected fruit bat origin requiring rapid WHO deployment.

key_facts: Endemic in West Africa with seasonal peaks. Multimammate rat (Mastomys) primary reservoir. Most infections asymptomatic.
response_summary: Enhanced surveillance during seasonal peaks. Healthcare worker training on infection prevention. Laboratory network strengthened.
lessons_learned: Endemic pathogens require sustained surveillance systems. Rodent control challenging in rural settings. Healthcare capacity building essential.
context_snippet: Lassa fever persists as an endemic hemorrhagic fever in West Africa with seasonal peaks, primarily spread through contact with infected Mastomys rodents.

key_facts: Largest cholera outbreak in modern history. Driven by collapsed healthcare infrastructure and water systems due to conflict.
response_summary: Humanitarian organizations provided oral rehydration therapy and water treatment. Vaccination campaigns in high-risk areas.
lessons_learned: Conflict settings create ideal conditions for waterborne disease outbreaks. Humanitarian access essential for outbreak response.
context_snippet: Conflict-driven collapse of water and sanitation infrastructure in Yemen triggered the largest cholera outbreak in recorded history with millions of suspected cases.

key_facts: Vector-borne viral disease affecting humans and livestock. Associated with heavy rainfall and flooding. Mosquito-borne transmission primary route.
response_summary: Livestock vaccination campaigns implemented. Vector control measures in affected areas. Cross-border surveillance enhanced.
lessons_learned: Climate patterns influence vector-borne disease emergence. One Health approach essential for livestock and human cases.
context_snippet: Rift Valley fever emerged in Rwanda following heavy rainfall, affecting both livestock and humans with significant mortality via mosquito and direct animal contact transmission.

key_facts: Biological Weapons Convention prohibits development, production, stockpiling of biological agents as weapons. 183 states parties as of 2023.
response_summary: Review conferences held every 5 years. Confidence building measures encourage transparency in biological research activities.
lessons_learned: Dual-use research oversight requires international coordination. Enforcement mechanisms limited but normative framework strong.
context_snippet: The Biological Weapons Convention is the primary international treaty prohibiting biological weapons, covering development, production, and stockpiling by 183 signatory states.

key_facts: US regulation of dangerous pathogens and toxins covering 67 agents across bacteria, viruses, fungi, and toxins. Requires registration, security, and personnel screening.
response_summary: CDC and USDA jointly administer program. Regular inspections and incident reporting required. Enhanced security post-2001 anthrax attacks.
lessons_learned: Laboratory security requires multi-layered approach. Personnel reliability programs essential component. Balance needed between security and research.
context_snippet: The US Select Agent Program regulates access to 67 dangerous pathogens and toxins, requiring registration, physical security, and personnel reliability screening.

key_facts: International Health Regulations require countries to develop core public health capacities for surveillance and response. PHEIC declaration authority vested in WHO.
response_summary: 196 countries committed to implementation. Joint external evaluations assess national capacities. Emergency committee advises on PHEIC declarations.
lessons_learned: Global health security requires national capacity building. Tension between sovereignty and international cooperation remains a challenge.
context_snippet: The International Health Regulations bind 196 countries to build surveillance and response capacities, and grant WHO the authority to declare Public Health Emergencies of International Concern.

key_facts: Guidelines for screening DNA synthesis orders to prevent biosecurity risks. Industry self-regulation with government oversight in some jurisdictions.
response_summary: Industry consortium (IGSC) develops best practices. Some countries implementing mandatory screening requirements.
lessons_learned: Technological advances require proactive governance frameworks. Public-private partnership models show promise for dual-use oversight.
context_snippet: The IGSC and national bodies provide guidance for screening commercial DNA synthesis orders to prevent misuse of gene synthesis technologies for biosecurity threats.

key_facts: Export control regime for dual-use biological equipment and materials. 42 participating countries coordinate restrictions on sensitive technologies.
response_summary: Regular updates to control lists based on technological developments. Information sharing among participating governments.
lessons_learned: Export controls can limit proliferation but require international coordination. Technology advancement outpaces regulatory frameworks.
context_snippet: The Australia Group coordinates export controls on dual-use biological materials and equipment among 42 countries to limit proliferation of biological weapons precursors.
